Abstract
--------

This article explores the rise of populism and its impact on democratic institutions through a comparative analysis of global trends, using a qualitative methodology based on literature review and library research. Populism, characterized by its anti-elite rhetoric and appeal to "the people," has gained significant traction across diverse political landscapes in recent years, affecting both mature and emerging democracies. The study examines the ways in which populist leaders and movements challenge key democratic principles, such as the separation of powers, judicial independence, and the integrity of the media. Drawing on case studies from various regions, including Europe, Latin America, and the United States, the research identifies common patterns in the populist approach to governance, particularly in the undermining of checks and balances and the erosion of institutional trust. Furthermore, this paper discusses how populism capitalizes on economic inequality, political disenfranchisement, and cultural tensions to gain popular support. The findings suggest that while populism poses significant risks to democratic institutions, its long-term impact varies depending on the strength and resilience of a country's legal and institutional framework. This study concludes with recommendations for safeguarding democratic governance in the face of rising populist movements, emphasizing the importance of reinforcing democratic norms, enhancing civic education, and ensuring institutional transparency.

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democrati c 

Institutions: A Comparative Analysis of Glo bal Trends 

Ali Nurdin 1, Asep Setiawan 2, Agus Rahmat Nugraha 3

Universitas Mathla’ul Anwar, Banten 1, Universitas Muhammadiyah Jakarta 2, 

STAIDA Muhammadiyah Garut 3, Indonesia 

Email: alinurdi n@unmab anten.ac.id, asep.s etiawan@umj.ac.i d, agusstai dam72@g mail.com

Keywords 

Abstract 

Populism, Democratic 

Institutions, Global 

Trends, Political 

Disenfranchisement, 

Comparative Analysis 

This article explores th e rise of pop ulism and its impact on d emocratic 

institutions through a comparative analysis of global trends, using a 

qualitative methodology based on literature review and library research. 

Populism, characterized by its anti-elite rhetoric and appeal to "the 

people," has gained significant traction across diverse political landscapes 

in recent years, affecting both mature and emergi ng democracies. The 

study examines the ways in which populist leaders and movements 

challenge key democratic principles, such as the separation of powers, 

judicial independence, and the integrity of the media. Drawing on case 

studies from various regions, including Europe, Latin America, and the 

United States, the research ident ifies common patterns in the populist 

approach to governance, particula rly in the undermining of checks and 

balances and the erosion of institutional trust. Furthermore, this paper 

discusses how populism capitalizes on economic inequality, political 

disenfranchisement, and cultural tensions to gain popular support. The 

findings suggest that while populism poses significant risks to democratic 

institutions, its long-term impact varies depend ing on the strength and 

resilience of a country's legal and institutional framework. This study 

concludes with recomme ndations for safeguarding democratic governance 

in the face of rising populist mo vements, emphasizing the impor tance of 

reinforcing democratic norms, enhancing civic education, and ensuring 

institutional transparency. 

INTRODUCTION 

The rise of populism has become a d efining feature of global politics in the 21st century, 

with populist leaders and movements gaining prominence across a wide array of democratic 

contexts. Populism, characterized by its anti-elite rhetoric, focus on direc t appeals to “the people,” 

68

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democra tic Institutions: A Comparative Analysis of Gl obal Trends 

and skepticism of established political institutions, presents a unique challenge to the functioning 

of democratic systems (Mudde, 2 017). Over the past deca de, populist leaders have risen to power 

in countries as diverse as the United States, Brazil, Hungary, and the Philippines, each adapting 

populist strategies to their local political and cultural environments (Norris &Inglehart, 2019). This 

phenomenon has raised critical questions about the resilience of democratic institutions and the 

extent to which populism may undermine or reshape democratic governance. 

Despite the growing body of literature examining populism, a key resea rch gap remains in 

understanding its comparative impact on democratic institutions across different regions. Much 

of the existing scholarship has focused on specific national contexts, such as the United States or 

Europe, w ithout sufficiently analyzing broader, global patterns (Mounk, 2018). Moreover, while 

many studies have explored the ideological roots of populism and its relationship to econ omic 

inequality, fewer have examined how pop ulist leaders interact with and alter institutional 

frameworks like the judiciary, media, and legislature (Rooduijn, 20 19). Addressing this gap is 

critical for understanding the broader, t ransnational effects of populism and the varying degrees 

of institutional vulnerability or resilience in different democracies. 

This research is urgently needed as populism continues to spread, with potentially long-

lasting consequences for democratic governance. The COVID-19 pandemic, economic crises, and 

rising inequality have further f ueled populist narra tives, intensifying th eir appeal in both 

established and emerging democracies (Kyle &Mounk, 2021). As populist leaders often frame 

themselves as defenders of "the people" against corrupt elites, t heir governance strategies 

frequently involve undermining democratic norms, such as judicial indepe ndence, press freedom,

and checks and balances (Levitsky &Ziblatt, 2018). These actions not only weaken institutional 

safeguards but also erode public trust in democracy itself, making the study of populism’s impact 

on democratic institutions particularly urgent. 

Previous studies on populism have provided valuable insights into the characteristics and 

rhetoric of populist movements. Mudde (2017) emphasized th e ideological fluidity of p opulism, 

which allows it to manifest in both left- and right-wing forms, while Müller (2016) highlighted its 

anti-pluralist tendencies that challenge the diversity of opinion inherent in democratic societies. 

However, a more comprehensive, comparative analysis is necessary to assess how populist 

governments have altered democratic institutions on a global scale. This study aims to fill this gap 

by comparing case st udies from multiple regio ns, providing a broader understanding of ho w 

populism interacts with institutional frameworks worldwide. 

Populism has surged across the globe in recent decades, transforming the political 

landscapes of both established and emerging democracies. Defined b y its appeal to "the people" 

and opposition to perceived elites, populism often frames itself as a correcti ve to corrupt or 

ineffective political systems (Mudde, 2017). Populist leaders typically claim to represent the true 

will of the people, positioning themselves against institutions such as the judiciary, media, and 

legislatures that they argue are aligned with elite interests. This antagonism toward institutions is 

a defining feature of populism and poses sig nificant challenges to the integrity of democratic 

69

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democratic In stitutions: A Comparative Analysis of Global Tr ends 

governance. 

The impact of populism on democrat ic institutions is profound and multifaceted. Populist 

leaders often seek to undermine key pillars of democracy, such as the separation of powers, 

judicial independence, and freedom of the press. In many cases, they attempt t o weaken checks 

and balances by appointing loyalists to the judiciary, restricting media freedom, and bypassing 

legislative processes (Levitsky &Ziblatt, 2018). These actions erode institutional safeguards 

designed to protect democracy from authoritarian tendencies, creating an environment w here

democratic norms are at risk. The concentration of power in the exe cutive branch, seen in 

countries like Hungary and Turkey, exemplifies how populist governance can lead to the gradual 

dismantling of democratic structures. 

However, the impact of p opulism varies depending on the strength of a country’s 

democratic institutions and it s political culture. In countries with resilient legal f rameworks and 

active civil society, such as the Unit ed States and some Western European na tions, popu list

leaders face significant resistance from independent institutions, media, and public opinion. In 

contrast, in nations with weaker institutions or histories of authoritarianism, populism can more 

easily dismantle democratic checks, leading to democrat ic backsliding or even autocracy (Norris 

&Inglehart, 2019). As populism continues to rise, its long-term effects on democracy remain a 

critical issue for global politics. 

The novelty of this research lies in i ts comparative approach, examining the impact of 

populism n ot just within a single national context but across d ifferent types of democracies. By 

analyzing populist movements in both mature democracies, such as the United States and 

Western Europe, and in newer or more fragile democracies, such as those in Eastern Europe and 

Latin America, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of ho w institutional strength and 

political culture mediate the effects of populism. Th is comparative analysis will also highlight 

commonalities in populist governance strategies and their implications for the future of 

democracy globally. 

The primary objective of this study is to assess the impact of populism on democratic 

institutions, focusing on how populist leaders undermine or reshape key demo cratic principles, 

including judicial independence, media freedom, and the separation of power s. Additionally, the 

study seeks to iden tify the factors that contribute to either the erosion or resilience of these 

institutions in different political contexts. The findings of this resear ch are expected to provide 

valuable insights for policymakers, scholars, and civil societ y organizations working to safeguard 

democratic governance. By identifying the mechanisms through which populism threatens 

democratic institutions, this research will offer practical recommendations for reinforcing 

institutional checks and balances a nd promoting democratic resilience in the face of rising populist 

movements. 

70

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democra tic Institutions: A Comparative Analysis of Gl obal Trends 

METHODS 

This study adopts a qualitative research methodology, specifically utilizing a literature-

based study (library research) to explore the rise of populism and its i mpact on democratic 

institutions through a comparative analysis of global trends. A literature review approach is 

employed, which is well-suited to examining theoretical frameworks, historical developments, 

and scholarly interpretations of complex political phenomena such as populism. This method 

enables a systematic examination of existing research and case studies from diverse regions, 

including Europe, Latin America, and the United States, to identify patterns i n how populism 

affects democratic governance (Snyder, 2019). 

The data sources for th is research consist of peer-reviewed journal articles, books, policy 

reports, and relevant legal documents. Primary data includes academic literature on populism, 

democratic theory, and political institutions, while secondary sources include analyses of populist 

movements and their effects on specific countries. Leading databases such as JSTOR, Scopus, and 

Google Scholar are used to collect relevant materials. Furthermore, reputable international 

organizations like Freedom House and the Economist Intelligence Uni t provide additional data on 

the state of democracy in various regions. The selection criteria prioritize recent publications 

(within the last decade), highly cited works, and those with a direct focus on the intersection of 

po pulism and democratic institutions (Booth et al., 2016). 

For data collection, a systematic literature review process is conducted, involving k eyword 

searches such as "populism and democracy," "impact of populism on institutions," and 

"comparative political analysis." Relevant articles are selecte d based on their methodological 

rigor, theoretical relevance, and c ontribution to understanding global tr ends in populism. The 

data is then organized by region and by thematic focus, allowing for a comprehensive comparative 

analysis of populism’s varying impacts on instit utional frameworks in different political contexts. 

The data analysis employs thematic analysis, which allows for the identification and 

interpretation of recurring themes related to populism’s effect on democratic institutions (Braun 

&Clarke, 2006). Key themes include the undermining of judicial independence, the erosion of 

media freedom, a nd the centralization of executive power. By categorizing the literature into 

these core themes, this study provides a s tructured analysis of the global trends and regio nal 

differences in how populism reshapes democratic institutions. The analysis seeks to identify both 

commonalities and divergences in populist strategies and their institutional consequences, 

offering a nuanced understanding of how populism interacts with various political systems. 

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION 

In conductin g this literature review on The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democratic 

Institutions: A Comparative Analys is of Global Trends, a thorough search was performed acros s 

academic databases such as Scopus, JSTOR, and Google Scholar. The research process identified 

numerous articles, books, and reports that address t he relationship between populism and its 

impact on democratic institutions. To ensure relevance and depth, 10 key articles were selected 

71

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democratic In stitutions: A Comparative Analysis of Global Tr ends 

based on their focus on the effects of populism on democratic frameworks, the challenges pose d to 

institutions such as the judiciary, media, and legislatures, and their comparative analysis acros s 

various regions. These articles were chosen from a larger pool of resources due to t heir rigorous 

methodology, scholarly impact, and the br eadth of insights they provide i nto populism’s global rise. 

The table below summarizes these selected art icles, highlighting their main findings and 

contributions to the research.

The table summarizes the 10 key sources selected for the literature review on populism's 

impact on democratic institutions. These articles provide a comprehensive overview of the 

theoretical frameworks, case studies, and regional analyses that inform the study. The works of 

Mudde (2017) and Levitsky & Ziblatt (2018) provide a found ational understanding of populism and 

its mechanisms for eroding democratic n orms, while regional studies by Pappas (2019) and Norris 

&Inglehart (2019) offer insights into how populism operates differently across political systems. 

Together, these sources form the core of the comparative analysis conducted in this research. 

The selected literature presents a comprehensive understanding of h ow populism, through 

its anti-elite and anti-establishment rhetoric, fundamentally c hallenges democratic institutions. One 

of the key findings across the reviewe d articles is that populism often thrives by positioning itself as 

a response to a perceiv ed disconnect between elites and ordinary citizens (Mudde, 2 017). This 

appeal to "the people" is central to the populist ideology and is effective in undermining democratic 

institutions that are viewed as part of the elite establishment. Institutions such as the judiciary, the

media, and legislatures are often framed by populists as corrupt or biased, paving the way for 

leaders to erode these institutions' independence in favor of consolidating power (Levitsky &Ziblatt, 

2018). 

Another major theme evident in the literature is the link between populism and economic 

and political disenfranchisement. M ounk (2018) and Norris &In glehart (2019) highlight how 

economic inequality and political marginalization have created fertile ground for populist 

movements to gai n momentum. Populist leaders exploit these grievances by presenting themselve s 

as the true representatives of the marginalized, offering simplistic solutions to complex problems. 

The result is often a weakening of institutional checks and balances, as populist governments claim 

that established institutions are part of the problem rather than the solution. This dynamic is 

especially pr onounced i n regio ns where the public feels disconnected from traditional political

structures. 

Cultural factors also play a significant role in the ri se of populism, as highlighted by Norris &

Inglehart (2019). Their analysis reveals that populism is not only driven b y econ omic or political 

factors but also by cultural shifts, particularly in resp onse to globalization, immigration, and 

perceived threats to national identity. These cultural anxieties have fueled populist movem ents 

across Europe and the Americas, where populist lea ders capitalize on f ears of cultural erosion to 

74

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democra tic Institutions: A Comparative Analysis of Gl obal Trends 

gain support. In these cases, populism often leads to a retreat from pluralism, as leaders reject 

diversity in favor of a homogeneous national identity, further eroding democratic principles (Müller, 

2016). 

Populist leaders in power often use crises to further consolidate control, as demonst rated 

by Kyle & Mounk (2021). Their study on the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic shows how populist 

leaders capitalized on the crisis to justify the expansion of executive power and th e weakening of 

institutional checks. This use of emergencies to centralize authority is a common tactic among 

populists, who argue that traditional democratic processes are too slow or ineffective to deal with 

crises. The pandemic, therefore, served as a pretext for populist leaders to further u ndermine 

democratic institutions by bypassing legislatures and weakening judicial oversight. 

The case studies from So uthern Europe, as analyzed by Pappas (2019), demonstrate t he real-

world consequences of populist governance on democratic institutions. In countries like Greece and 

Italy, populist governments systematically dismantled institutional safeguards, such as judicial 

independence and media freedom, while consolidating their own power. This regional analysis 

underscores the broader global trends observed in the literature: populists in power often prioritize 

personal or party loyalty over institutional integrity, leading to long-term damage to democratic 

governance. These findings suggest that populism's impact is most pronounced in democracies 

where institutions are already weakened or un der-resourced, making them more vulnerable to 

populist manipulation. 

The literature reveals that while populism can emerge as a corrective response to political 

and economic disenfranchisement, its impact on democratic i nstitutions is largely negative. The 

common patterns identified—such as the erosion of checks and balances, u ndermining of judicial 

independence, and weakening of media freedom—highlight the risks populism poses to democrati c 

governance. However, the degree to which populism undermines democracy varies depending on 

the strength of a country's institutional framework. In nations with resilient institutions, populism 

may pose a temporary challenge, but in more fragile democracies, the damage can be far-reaching 

and long-lasting. 

The findings from the literature review reveal critical insights into how populism influences 

and reshapes democratic institutions across diverse political contexts. A common thread in the 

research is the ability of populist movements to exploit societal divisions—whether economic,

cultural, or political—and channel them into a broad challenge against established institutions 

(Mudde, 2017). This is particularly evident in populist rhetoric that frames traditional democratic 

structures, such as the judiciary and the media, as c orrupt or disconnected from the needs of "the 

people" (Levitsky & Ziblatt, 2018). This erosion of trust in institutions is a recurring phenomenon 

in countries where populist leaders have risen to power, and it aligns with contemporary global 

trends where democracy is increasingly seen as vulnerable to populist challenges. 

Recent global events, such as the COVID-19 pandemic, have amplified the influence of 

populist leaders, allowing them to consolidate power by leveraging crises. Kyle & Mounk (2021) 

75

The Rise of Populism and Its Impact on Democratic In stitutions: A Comparative Analysis of Global Tr ends 

demonstrate how populist leaders utilized the pandemic to justify bypassing democratic checks 

and accelerating execu tive control. This pattern is reflected in c ountries like H ungary, where Prim e 

Minister Viktor Orbán used the pandemic as a justification to rule by decree, sidelining the national 

parliament. This confirms the theoretical perspec tive that populism thrives in environments where 

crises allow leaders to weaken institutional oversight under the pretext of swift governance 

(Mounk, 2018). 

Economic inequality is another significant driver of populist support. Many scholars, such 

as Norris &Inglehar t (2019), point to the economic disenfranchisement experienced by larg e 

segments of the population as a key factor fueling populist movements. This is particularly relevant 

in the current global climate, where growing disparities between eli tes and the working class have

led t o widespread dissatisfaction. For instance, populist leaders like Donald Trump in the U.S. or 

Jair Bolsonaro in Brazil gained support by tapping into feelings of economic insecurity and 

frustration with traditional political

SUMMARY:
The argument that populist incumbents threaten institutions of democratic contestation—and, less frequently, that they correct some aspects of political participation and representation—has been made from multiple approaches.1 Scholars and commentators often cite specific cases of populists harming democracy and, since 2016, several large-N studies have confirmed their negative impact. The argument that populist incumbents threaten institutions of democratic contestation—and, less frequently, that they correct some aspects of political participation and representation—has been made from multiple approaches.Footnote 1 Scholars and commentators often cite specific cases of populists harming democracy and, since 2016, several large-N studies have confirmed their negative impact. Populism and the Two Faces of Democracy.” Political Studies 47 (1): 2–16.CrossRefGoogle Scholar

Extract
-------

Populism’s effects on democracy after populists gain control of government (hereafter, populist incumbents) are some of the best theorized and documented consequences. The argument that populist incumbents threaten institutions of democratic contestation—and, less frequently, that they correct some aspects of political participation and representation—has been made from multiple approaches.1 Scholars and commentators often cite specific cases of populists harming democracy and, since 2016, several large-N studies have confirmed their negative impact. Specifically, studies repeatedly show the harmful effects of populist incumbents on civil liberties, including media freedom, horizontal accountability, and electoral integrity in both electoral and liberal democracies. Research has been less consistent in showing the positive consequences of populist incumbents, especially for democratic representation and political participation.

* * *

Information
-----------

 Type Populism and Democracy: Mapping the Field and the Road Ahead

 Information 

[PS: Political Science & Politics](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics) , [Volume 58](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/volume/91604696744E1E431BA81EE969A7BBD8) , [Issue 1](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/issue/D36A60B213922F2F436AD194CF8BBEF1), January 2025, pp. 77 - 81

* * *

Populism’s effects on democracy after populists gain control of government (hereafter, populist incumbents) are some of the best theorized and documented consequences. The argument that populist incumbents threaten institutions of democratic contestation—and, less frequently, that they correct some aspects of political participation and representation—has been made from multiple approaches.[Footnote 1](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#en1) Scholars and commentators often cite specific cases of populists harming democracy and, since 2016, several large-N studies have confirmed their negative impact. Specifically, studies repeatedly show the harmful effects of populist incumbents on civil liberties, including media freedom, horizontal accountability, and electoral integrity in both electoral and liberal democracies. Research has been less consistent in showing the positive consequences of populist incumbents, especially for democratic representation and political participation.

Although this literature reflects a significant accumulation of scholarly research, at least two overlooked opportunities for further study exist. First, researchers too frequently rely on narrow regional analyses and use imprecise measures for key variables. We call these _missed empirical opportunities._ Second, scholars have been slow to compare and test competing approaches to populism, especially the ideational and political–strategic approaches. We call this a _missed scientific opportunity._

EXISTING APPROACHES
-------------------

Although this special issue focuses on the ideational approach, the literature on populism and liberal democracy draws heavily from a second, competing approach: the political–strategic approach. Without considering both approaches, it can be difficult to understand the design and the implications of current studies.[Footnote 2](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#en2)

The ideational approach defines populism as a thin-centered ideology or a discourse that frames politics as a struggle between the will of the common people and an evil, conspiring elite (Hawkins et al. [Reference Hawkins, Carlin, Littvay and Kaltwasser 2019](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r11)). This approach tends to see populist incumbents having two types of impact. On the one hand, leaders who use this rhetoric may have a positive impact on democratic participation and representation because of their efforts to reshape the political agenda and to build new institutions of democratic inclusion (Canovan [Reference Canovan 1999](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r4); Mény and Surel [Reference Mény and Surel 2002](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r26)). Populist incumbents make these efforts because of their belief in popular sovereignty and because populist forces are arising in response to actual failures of liberal democracy to fully represent the electorate. In this way, the emergence of populism can be a corrective for democracy (Mudde and Rovira Kaltwasser [Reference Mudde and Kaltwasser 2012](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r27)).

On the other hand, populist incumbents consistently harm democratic contestation and other core institutions of liberal democracy (Abts and Rummens [Reference Abts and Rummens 2007](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r1); Canovan [Reference Canovan 1999](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r4); Mény and Surel [Reference Mény and Surel 2002](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r26); Mudde and Rovira Kaltwasser [Reference Mudde and Kaltwasser 2012](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r27); Urbinati [Reference Urbinati 2019](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r39)). Generally, the literature highlights three mechanisms. First, because populist leaders and their followers perceive democracy as the expression of what ordinary people would want if they had unbiased information and could think freely of elite influence—a popular will that populists believe they know and alone embody—populist leaders are more willing than non-populists to violate norms and rules of electoral competition once they are in office (Abts and Rummens [Reference Abts and Rummens 2007](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r1), [Reference Abts and Rummens 417](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r1); Müller [Reference Müller 2016](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r29); Ochoa Espejo [Reference Ochoa Espejo and de la Torre 2015](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r30), [Reference Ochoa Espejo and de la Torre 61](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r30)). Second, because populists see their opponents as powerful agents who knowingly conspire against the common good, they believe they must curtail their civil liberties and make them pay for their crimes, even at the expense of due process. Third, because electorally successful populist forces are often personalistic movements (Pappas [Reference Pappas 2016](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r32)), followers are frequently motivated to concentrate government powers in the hands of their chief executive, whom they see as the embodiment of their will. Thus, populist incumbents undermine horizontal accountability.

In contrast to the ideational approach, the political–strategic approach views populist incumbents as having a more consistently negative impact on liberal democracy—indeed, on the same institutions mentioned by ideational theorists. This approach explains this negative impact not as a function of populist ideas but instead by what it sees as the defining features of populist forces: a personalistic organization led by charismatic political outsiders (Barr [Reference Barr 2017](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r2); Levitsky and Loxton [Reference Levitsky and Loxton 2013](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r24)). Again, three mechanisms are posited. First, because populists are often political outsiders, they experience high costs in building relationships with traditional political elites. Hence, once in power, they prefer to undermine the party system and rebuild political institutions from the constitution up (Levitsky and Loxton [Reference Levitsky and Loxton 2013](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r24)). Second, because populist forces are personalistic movements, once in power, their leaders find it easier and simpler to dismantle checks and balances in favor of the executive branch, and they need control of the media to communicate with their followers (Kenny [Reference Kenny 2020](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r20); Weyland [Reference Weyland 2024](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r41)). Third, as vote-maximizing politicians, populist incumbents are keen on satisfying constituents who voted them into office on a platform of radical institutional change and “cleaning house.” Indeed, the support of large numbers of voters gives them the power to carry out this mandate (Levitsky and Loxton [Reference Levitsky and Loxton 2013](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r24)).

RESULTS: POPULISM AS THREAT
---------------------------

In recent years, empirical studies from both approaches have consistently confirmed the broad claims of a negative association between populism and democratic contestation/liberal democracy. Early research on the impact of populism on democracy often featured individual country or small-N studies (e.g., Betz [Reference Betz 2001](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r3); de la Torre [Reference de la Torre 1997](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r7); Hawkins [Reference Hawkins 2003](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r10)). However, the literature made an important step forward with the publication of Mudde and Rovira Kaltwasser’s ([Reference Mudde and Kaltwasser 2012](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r27)) book, which not only included theoretical contributions from the ideational side but also was one of the first to compare populism across regions. Contributors found that contemporary populists in Europe and Latin America were associated with declines in democratic contestation, although these effects were moderated by the prior strength of democratic institutions. Another early comparative study from the political–strategic approach was Levitsky and Loxton’s ([Reference Levitsky and Loxton 2013](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r24)) article on populism and democracy in Latin America. In an analysis of several contemporary governments in the region, they argued that populist outsiders were associated with significant declines in electoral quality, civil liberties of opponents, and horizontal accountability. It is important that they noted the primacy of elections and plebiscites, which grant populists the legitimacy to weaken democratic institutions, ultimately leading to comparative authoritarian regimes. Similar findings emerged in a study of Latin America by de la Torre and Arnson ([Reference de la Torre and Arnson 2013](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r8)).

Since 2016, researchers have confirmed these findings with larger datasets using broad indicators of democratic contestation. In two seminal large-N studies, Huber and Schimpf ([Reference Huber and Schimpf 2016a](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r14), [Reference Huber and Schimpf 2016b](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r15)) found that populist incumbents were associated with overall declines in democracy (measured in terms of the Polity IV polity2 measure) in both Latin America and Europe. Three cross-regional studies—Kyle and Mounk (Polity IV, 2018), Juon and Bochsler (Democracy Barometer, 2020), and Ruth-Lovell and Grahn (Varieties of Democracy, 2023)—also found that populism was associated with declines in liberal democracy. Most of these studies based their hypotheses on the ideational approach.

Other research focused on specific institutions of liberal democracy, including media freedom, the rule of law, and the strength of the constraints placed on the executive’s powers (i.e., horizontal accountability). In this instance, a few studies (Kenny [Reference Kenny 2017](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r19), [Reference Kenny 2020](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r20)) drew more clearly from the political–strategic approach. Huber and Schimpf ([Reference Huber and Schimpf 2017](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r16)) found that populism in Europe was particularly harmful to horizontal accountability. Houle and Kenny ([Reference Houle and Kenny 2018](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r12)) followed this finding with a study showing that populism was associated with declines in horizontal accountability, judicial freedom, and the rule of law in Latin America, which Kenny ([Reference Kenny 2017](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r19)) confirmed with a global dataset. In a Latin American study, Ruth ([Reference Ruth 2018](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r33)) showed the conditions under which populism was more likely to lead to declines in horizontal accountability. Finally, Kenny ([Reference Kenny 2020](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r20)) extended his 2017 analysis with a cross-regional study on the negative impact on media freedom. It is important to note that many of these studies demonstrate that these negative effects are general to populism of the left and right (e.g., Huber and Schimpf [Reference Huber and Schimpf 2017](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r16); Juon and Bochsler [Reference Juon and Bochsler 2020](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/article/effect-of-populist-incumbents-on-democracy/87A3DE4CAAF63BC0BCCE3A9990368EEC#r18); Kyle and Mounk [Reference Kyle and Mounk 2018](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/ps-political-science-and-politics/art

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Impact of governance on populist rhetoric ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0939362524000682

SUMMARY:
by F Reck · 2025 · Cited by 2 — We find that effective governance reduces populist rhetoric. Refugee immigration and one-party dominance, on the other hand, increase populist rhetoric.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Strong political institutions mitigate populism's negative effects by maintaining checks and balances, enhancing accountability, and ensuring that democratic norms are upheld despite populist pressures.
Political culture shapes responses to populism; resilient democratic values promote resistance to populism, while cultures with deep-seated mistrust in institutions facilitate populist narratives.
Case studies from various regions reveal that populism's impact varies; effective governance and robust institutions can counteract populist rhetoric, offering pathways to safeguard democracy.
